Description: Review Current batches branch and merge auto rotate pull request

SwinOCR A/B model loading refactor (config-driven dual path) Model factory: Replaced single build_model() with build_or_load_model(cfg[''model'']) in models/swin_ocr.py. Routing by decoder_type in config (''subword'' vs ''char''). Path 1 (subword): Load Swin+AraGPT2 + AutoTokenizer from local checkpoint when checkpoint_dir exists; otherwise build from HF. All from config. Path 2 (char): “Frankenstein” build: load ArabicCharTokenizer and trained char decoder from old ViT-TrOCR checkpoint (old_char_model_dir), fresh Swin from encoder_name, stitch into SwinOCRModel with bridge IDs and cross-attention config.

SwinOCR Arabic OCR pipeline – encoder research, Swin integration, training & inference Encoder research: Evaluated encoders for Arabic multi-word OCR; chose Swin over ViT to avoid square-input constraints and 2D→1D flattening that caused hallucinations. Architecture: Swin-Base encoder + AraGPT2 decoder, with a custom 2D-to-1D height-pooling bridge (mean over height, then RTL flip) for RTL Arabic alignment. Preprocessing: Implemented pad_for_swin (aspect-ratio-preserving resize, right-side padding, dimensions divisible by 32). Training: Added train.py with Seq2SeqTrainer, config-driven setup, stratified splits, CER/WER metrics, gradient checkpointing, and fp16. Inference: Added inference.py for single-image and batch prediction with beam search and repetition controls. Sanity checks: Visual padding check, forward-pass shape check, and overfit-on-one-batch test. Project layout: Modular structure under SwinOCR/ (config, dataset, metrics, models). Environment: Conda env and requirements.txt for reproducible setup.

Built a debuggable DBNet v5 Arabic word-cropping pipeline: raw detections → filter → merge fragments (fix single-word split into 2 boxes) → split wide boxes into words (projection + guard rails + optional recursion) → RTL-sorted word crops + JSON manifest.  Added stage-by-stage visualization saving (dbnet_stage_tracking.py) so every step can be inspected and thresholds tuned independently (filtered boxes, merge result, split candidates, per-split debug artifacts, final output).  Replaced the Canny approach with a morphology-based table border removal (opening + optional dilation) to reduce line/border interference during word splitting, tuned via kernel fractions/mins and dilation iters.

Implemented and iteratively tuned a new DBNet v5 post-processing pipeline to improve Arabic word-level detection: added score/size filtering, projection-profile based splitting of wide boxes, table-line removal with morphology, smart padding, and RTL reading-order sorting to generate cleaner per-word crops. In parallel, worked on enhancing the custom TrOCR model at sentence level by refining tokenizer/vocab handling, generation settings (greedy decoding vs beams), and training configuration to better align recognition with the improved detection outputs.

Configured PaddleOCR DBNet v5 for Arabic handwriting (environment setup, verifying Paddle/PaddleOCR installation, and loading the PP-OCRv5_server_det model). Implemented a detection pipeline that runs DBNet over sentence level images, saves visual overlays, and exports per-box crops + a JSON manifest. Then added generic post-processing to improve word isolation: filtering noisy/low-score and tiny boxes, heuristically handling overly wide boxes, and adding padding around each crop so diacritics and edge characters are preserved before passing word crops to the TrOCR recognizer.

Researched possible options to extend the current Arabic handwriting OCR pipeline from word-level to sentence-level, including:  Training on synthetic 2-word sequences versus relying on external word segmentation.  Using a separate detection/segmentation model to split sentences into words and feeding them to the existing encoder–decoder.  Exploring alternative vision encoders (e.g., Swin-based encoders) to better handle longer input images.  Analysed the behavior of the current architecture on sentence-like inputs and compared its limitations against the synthetic 2-word experiments.  Met with Dr. Ahmed to review the results of the two experiments, discuss the degradation at sentence level, and align on potential next steps (synthetic multi-word training, segmentation-first pipeline, and trying stronger encoders) for future iterations.

Implemented a Python script to generate a synthetic 2-word Arabic handwriting dataset by concatenating word-level images, enforcing RTL order and skipping pairs with identical ground-truth words.  Verified the integrity of the new dataset (image ↔ .gt.txt pairing, correct label formatting, ~3000 samples).  Developed and ran an evaluation script (eval_synthetic_2words.py) that loads the current TrOCR checkpoint, reuses the API inference pipeline (beam search + Hunspell), and computes CER/WER on the synthetic 2-word dataset using a custom Levenshtein implementation.  Confirmed that inference is running on GPU and saved detailed evaluation results (JSON + sample predictions) for later analysis of sentence-level behavior.
